data.title: ZTO Express: Parcel Boom Meets Margin Gloom
data.description:
ZTO Express (NYSE:ZTO) reported 1Q25 results, with revenue and non-GAAP earnings below consensus. Although volume was largely inline with expectations, the softer-than-expected ASP drove much of the miss and partially illustrates the growing competition within China as logistics companies cut prices to maintain volume growth. Management reiterated its full-year parcel volume target for 2025, but we believe the narrative could play out similarly in Q1, where topline growth is being held up by rising volume, which is offset by lower ASPs.

With China’s macroeconomic environment remaining uncertain due to the weak housing market and corporate sentiment, as well as the precarious Sino-US trade tension, we remain cautious about economically sensitive stocks such as ZTO and will stay on the sidelines until there is incremental clarity on the directional beta.

We like ZTO’s proven track record in the logistics sector, given its well-balanced incentives with network partners and operational efficiency of the self-operated fleet. Still, we would be more bullish on the name when the macro environment improves and the retail consumption picks up, given that ZTO’s volume is primarily driven by its ecommerce partners, such as Alibaba and PDD, that account for 2/3 of ZTO’s total volume.

On the flip side, we would be more cautious on ZTO should the consumption environment worsen. Note that our bullish thesis on PDD centralizes on China’s consumption downgrade trend where consumers continue to spend but at a smaller ticket size and forgo the branded items for white-label or practical items, which PDD specializes in, however, we would be concerned if the consumption downgrade trend shifts to consumption pullback, which is a major minus for ecommerce companies as it would translates to lower retail demand, thus lower order volume for ZTO. Additionally, given the expansion of JDL into TTG, we would also be cautious of potential competitive risk. The lower ASP may imply that ZTO had to sacrifice certain pricing in order to maintain order volume (see: JD Logistics: Powering JD Retail And Global Expansion).

We are neutral on this stock and rate this as a HOLD as we await further clarity on the macro and the competitive side.

ZTO currently trades at 6x forward EBITDA, which is largely in line with the peer average of 6.5x. If we were to value ZTO at exactly the peer average of 6.5x, we derive a value of $18/share, or 9% upside from the current level.

Total revenue of RMB 10.9bn was an increase of 9.4% y/y, which came in roughly 7% below consensus despite the 19% growth in parcel volume. Express delivery service revenue grew by 10% y/y, with ASP declining roughly 8%, while transportation and sortation cost per parcel declined by 9 cents in Q1. Signs of GPM compression were evident from the 540bps y/y decline to 24.7%, driving a 3ppt decline in EBITDA, which led to a miss in net profit.

Solid volume growth driving retail penetration

Daily retail parcel volume grew 46% y/y to 6mn parcels/day while reverse logistics grew >150% y/y to 3.5mn parcels/day, underscoring the strength of ZTO’s strategic partnership with e-commerce players. Given that ZTO focuses on low-value parcels, it partially avoided the costly price war, allowing it to drive higher market penetration. In addition, ZTO has managed to achieve incremental network scalability with direct linkage between 42.6% of outlets and last-mile posts, reduced delivery costs by RMB0.1/parcel, which enhances partner profitability and operational efficiency.

Management guided 2025 volume growth of 20%-24%, which translates to 40.8bn to 42.2bn parcels due to the rising demand for reverse logistics as the e-commerce return rate exceeded 30% in China.

We believe the volume target is at least sustainable, given that China has implemented a number of measures to boost consumption, as indicated by the positive retail sales growth. Notably, both Alibaba and JD have reported positive GMV growth this quarter, and this momentum is expected to accelerate into Q2, as early indicators from the 618 shopping festival have suggested strong e-commerce orders due to the ongoing subsidy program.

As for the rest of the year, the de-escalation of the Sino-US tension is likely to be incrementally positive for China as the policymakers can shift their attention to addressing the internal challenges that the country is facing, rather than being split between fixing both domestic issues and external issues.

Signs of pricing pressure remain

Core express ASP fell 7.8% y/y due to industry competition and a higher mix of lightweight parcels. We suspect that the integration of JDL into Alibaba may have led to increased competition and pricing pressure on ZTO. This decline in pricing has driven an outsized margin compression where gross margin contracted 5.4 ppts to 24.7%, despite the volume growth.

Management is keen on focusing on key accounts where the ASP improved by RMB 0.12/parcel due to higher volume, which mitigated the decline in ASP. However, we question whether this will offset the pricing pressure in the second half of the year.

To further mitigate the margins, management has also implemented route optimization and higher fleet utilization to drive greater cost efficiency, where transportation cost per parcel declined 13% y/y to RMB 0.41 and sorting cost per parcel declined 10% y/y to RMB 0.27 as ZTO adds increased automation across its warehouses.

We like these initiatives and believe these are inevitable trends, but we again question the sustainability in light of lower prices and possibly higher costs. We note that since JDL began accepting orders from Taobao and Tmall merchants, these merchants have more options for selecting a logistics partner for outbound shipments. In some cases, JDL is more cost-effective than ZTO.

While the exact price may vary by region, we note that JDL typically charges RMB 6.5 for the first kilogram and RMB 2 per kilogram for subsequent weight increases. This is cheaper than ZTO, which usually charges RMB 6-10 for the first kilogram and RMB 2/kg for the additional weight.

Given that ZTO focuses on the smaller parcels and JDL's small parcel pricing is slightly lower, we suspect that JDL may have taken some share from ZTO during the quarter.

Although we were encouraged by the growth in KA volume which has higher pricing, we are also cautious that without a clear macro recovery, the KA volume share may not increase enough to offset the broader decline in ASP, and the reverse logistics can only go so far to alleviate the margin pressure. If the aggressive pricing remains, we are doubtful of a sustained margin recovery.

Cloudy outlook with a mix of showers

Management highlighted several initiatives focusing on digitization, network, and product mix.

On the digitization side, AI integration is underway as machine vision is being utilized to reduce sorting errors, while a route optimization algorithm could cut transportation costs. Future plans include AI for last-mile delivery and autonomous vehicles, but we believe these will likely be a long-term project rather than anything in the near to medium term. On the bright side, automated barcode generation and AI-driven workflow tools improved sorting accuracy by 15% and employee efficiency by 20%.

Management has also been focused on direct outlet-to-last-mile linkages, which reduce delivery costs and improve partner retention, critical for sustaining volume growth across its networks.

Finally, management is optimistic about the reverse logistics that grew 150% y/y and retail parcels now represent ~30% of total volume, which could diversify revenue streams and reduce reliance on core express.

In short, we see the quarter as characterized by a mix of puts and takes, where pricing headwinds may offset sustained volume growth, and ZTO may be required to continue driving cost discipline and scaling KA/reverse logistics to protect margins. We need greater macro clarity before becoming bullish on the name, and recommend investors stay on the sidelines for now.

data.title: Marubeni: Undervalued And Buffett-Certified Japanese Trading House
data.description:
Since I last covered Marubeni (OTCPK:MARUY) in my piece entitled “Choose Buffet’s Japan Pick As Good Times May Be Over In America”, in March 2023, it has rallied by more than 35%.

Judging by the way investors seem to be rotating from the U.S. to international stocks, the Japanese trading house could sustain its gains as part of a broader market momentum, which has seen the MSCI World ex USA Index (which includes rest of the world stocks) outperforming the MSCI USA Index since the start of 2025, as charted below.

In such a context, this thesis aims to show that it is time to go international, and one of the reasons for choosing Marubeni is that Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), which had initially invested in the conglomerate in 2020, increased its stake in March this year. Thus, analyzing the risks, I identify compelling factors to buy the stock given the appeal of Japanese equities.

Japan's Appeal amid U.S. Uncertainty

First, checking whether the rotation away from the U.S. to the rest of the world also applies to the East Asian country, the chart below shows that U.S. stocks (MSCI USA) are indeed underperforming MSCI Japan since the beginning of April, somewhat coinciding with the announcement of reciprocal tariff on the rest of the world by the Trump administration.

However, looking in the past, it is the MSCI USA that has outperformed for the last ten years, has been driven by strong corporate earnings of the tech sector, given it has a sizeable (above 30%) "Magnificent Seven" holdings, which have been able to capture opportunities from the AI theme. Moreover, generally speaking, U.S. companies delivered solid earnings, supported by productivity gains, buybacks, a resilient consumer base, without forgetting a favorable international trade environment. Additionally, low inflation and a mostly accommodative Federal Reserve policy supported equity valuations.

However, 2025 has introduced structural shifts.

First, tariffs can potentially result in trade disruptions in case the U.S. does not manage to negotiate agreements with its partners during the 90-day truce. Worst, retaliatory measures by China, with whom tensions have again flared up recently, risk disrupting supply chains, raising input costs, and reducing corporate margins for sectors reliant on global trade. Thus, S&P 500's earnings are expected to increase by only 8.4% versus an estimate of 14% in January, and this does not consider potential tensions with the EU. By comparison, earnings improved by 12.1% in 2024.

Second, there could be inflationary pressures from potential tax cuts, which may reduce federal revenue by around $4.1 trillion over a decade and contribute to increasing the deficit. In addition, this can increase the disposable income of enterprises and people, leading to higher demand and culminating in higher inflation. As a result, the Fed may not be able to reduce rates as planned.

The combination of lower earnings and higher-for-longer rates could create further uncertainty for equities and undermine U.S. exceptionalism. Thus, while some analysts expect American stocks to remain strong, I believe Japan could continue gaining this year.

One of the reasons is the depreciating dollar, which may improve returns on Japanese investments. To this end, following weakness in the USD from 2003 to 2008, Japan outperformed America due to returns worth more when brought back home (from Japan).

Another reason for diversifying into Japan is renewed investor confidence, largely fueled by corporate governance reforms since 2019. Equally important, Japanese stocks in general are better priced when comparing the price-to-sales ratios of the iShares MSCI Japan ETF (EWJ) and the iShares MSCI USA Equal Weighted ETF (EUSA), making them attractive for value investors like Warren Buffett.

Marubeni's Strong Value Proposition and Profitability

In this case, despite the stock appreciating by 35% and its price-to-sales ratio surging from 0.35x to 0.66x, it is available at a discount of 57% relative to the industrials sector. Noteworthily, its P/S is lower than ITOCHU (OTCPK:ITOCY), another major Japanese trading house, by 15.4% ((0.66-0.78)/0.78) despite boasting higher revenue growth as pictured below. Thus, aligning its P/S on ITOCHU, I have a target of around $228 (197.5 x 1.154) for Marubeni based on the current share price of $197.5.

In addition to being undervalued, Marubeni generates significant cash from operations, which increased by 36% YoY in fiscal year 2025 ending in March this year, appealing this time to Buffett's preference for cash. Another enticing fact is that it pays dividends, which, for the Oracle of Omaha, is a "tangible representation of intrinsic value."

In this connection, Marubeni announced an annual dividend of 95 yen per share, as tabled below, a 10 yen increase from last year. Also, with a payout ratio of around 30-35%, it is balanced in its ability to reward shareholders while keeping sufficient earnings for growth purposes. Additionally, the company has improved buybacks fourfold over one year (from 20 billion yen to 80 billion yen), which could boost EPS, potentially driving up share value over time.

The above table also shows the net profit increasing by 31.6%, which is explained by Marubeni being a major Japanese trading and investment conglomerate that operates across a vast array of sectors, including food, agriculture & chemicals, energy and metals, power, transportation, and consumer products. Thus, it saw strong performance in the forest products segment (figure below), driven by higher pulp market prices. It also saw growth in the aerospace (aircraft parts) and shipping (ship owning) segments, while the U.K.-based power business also saw substantial profits. These managed to offset weakness in construction, industrial machinery, mobility, and food.

However, there are risks.

There are Risks, but Product Diversification is a positive

Marubeni faces uncertainty from Trump’s proposed tariffs on Japan because these are being imposed on items such as steel, aluminum, and automobiles. The problem is that these tariffs could raise the cost of Japanese exports to the U.S., thereby reducing demand for products where Marubeni is involved, potentially squeezing profit margins.

Thus, if tariffs are not lifted or exemptions are not granted, Marubeni, like other Japanese trading houses, could face indirect effects from a global slowdown, in case tariffs depress overall trade volumes and economic sentiment. This may be the reason why the stock suffered from a downside on April 2 from around $161 to $141, following the announcement of tariffs including 24% on Japanese imports. Still, it has recovered since then after tariffs were reduced to 10% and Japanese authorities showed the willingness to negotiate with their U.S. counterparts to find a trade solution.

Focusing on Marubeni, the management has been actively monitoring the situation and adjusting its investment and sourcing strategies accordingly. Furthermore, to manage these risks, Marubeni has set aside a 30 billion yen loss buffer to absorb some potential indirect impacts from U.S. tariffs. Thus, while Marubeni does not expect to be directly hit, it faces uncertainty from the effect of U.S. tariffs on economic growth, and any escalation remains a concern.

However, switching to a positive tone, Marubeni does not expect “significant and immediate” negative impact from U.S. tariffs, according to the Chief Executive Masayuki Omoto. To this end, the company expects net profits for FY-26 (which will end in March 2026) to rise by 7%, as shown below, and increase the dividend to 100 yen per share.

In the same vein, the company’s diversified portfolio and global supply chains, including investments in South Asian manufacturing and other non-U.S. markets, could help mitigate tariffs' effect by providing resilience against economic downturns. Another factor that plays in Marubeni's favor is that it owns the Helena Chemical Company, the second-largest agricultural chemical distributor in the U.S., which is de facto not subject to tariffs.

The Business Model is also Diversified and Emphasizes Cash Generation

Moreover, differentiating itself from other trading houses engaged mainly in import and export activities, Marubeni has diversified its business model to deliver project development and strategic investment activities in addition to trading and brokering. Thus, in addition to product sales and commissions, its revenue streams include service fees and investment returns as exemplified by its overseas subsidiary, Marubeni America Corporation, which acts as a major financier, investor, and organizer of global trade, providing specialized services such as logistics, insurance, and business consulting.

As a result, it can leverage its uniqueness by building on its strong global networks, strategic partnerships, and advanced logistics to manage risks.

Adding a note of caution, at the time of investing, Buffett took advantage of Japan's weaker currency and low inflation, but things have changed since then. Thus, after reaching a 30-year low of 160 yen per U.S. dollar in 2022, the yen has appreciated to approximately 142–144 yen per dollar as of early June 2025, while Japan's inflation has been on the rise, with core consumer prices in Tokyo surging by 3.6% year-over-year in May 2025. This may motivate the BoJ, which ended negative interest rates in March 2024, to further tighten monetary policy, weighing on Marubeni's finances given it had a total debt of $16.9 billion at the end of FY-25 versus only $3.8 billion of cash.

However, generating nearly $4 billion in cash from operations provides a solid buffer to meet interest payments, support working capital, and continue to invest, even during downturns. Also, while not liquid like cash, its large receivables base of $10.1 billion could be securitized to raise cash during difficult times. Additionally, its capital allocation strategy (an item which Buffett attaches significant importance to) represents strength in these uncertain times, given that it aims to optimize its business portfolio as shown below. In this regard, Marubeni aims to strengthen its cash generation capacity by accelerating divestments, with the proceeds redeployed into high-quality growth investments.

This focus on portfolio optimization to allocate resources more effectively enables streamlining of Marubeni's operations to concentrate on sectors with higher profitability. In this way, the net profit forecast for FY-26 includes an asset replacement gain/loss (since some divested assets may be sold at a gain, and others at a loss) of 80 billion yen.

Finally, Marubeni represents Buffett’s typical strategy: buying undervalued, cash-generating businesses with strong moats, while its shareholder-friendly moves, namely dividend payments and share buybacks, make it attractive in today’s uncertainty-ridden market for investors looking for returns abroad.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best International Stock Idea investment competition, which runs through June 6. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nvidia Back In Focus And AI Infrastructure Acceleration
data.description:
We distill the major insights from Nvidia’s earnings, exploring what’s changed in the dynamic semiconductor industry, what’s accelerating, and what it might mean for investors.

Each quarter, we break down the evolving world of semiconductors through the lens of Nvidia’s (NVDA) earnings and the broader AI investment landscape. This latest call came at a pivotal moment, with demand surging, CapEx accelerating, and the semiconductor ecosystem transforming faster than most expected.

Nvidia is “Back in the Game,” But It's Not Alone

While Nvidia remains the headline act, the semiconductor narrative is no longer a one-player story. After spending some time “on the bench,” Nvidia has returned to the spotlight, thanks to renewed demand, sovereign AI expansion, and robust margins on next-gen products. But the real story lies in the entire stack—from foundational GPU builders like Nvidia and Broadcom to the less visible but crucial chip designers in the fabless space.

What’s changed? For one, the pace. From model development to chip deployment, the speed of innovation and adoption, particularly across hyperscalers and enterprise infrastructure, has exceeded expectations. This confirms a core view we’ve held that diversified exposure, particularly through vehicles like the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) and the VanEck Fabless Semiconductor ETF (SMHX), is critical in capturing this sector’s momentum without betting on a single name.

1-Year Performance Snapshot: Nvidia Returns, but Broadcom Leads

CapEx: Not a Bubble, But a Baseline

A key theme this quarter was capital expenditure—its scale, sustainability, and strategic importance. Nvidia’s call emphasized sovereign AI deals (notably in the Middle East), rising enterprise demand, and hyperscalers racing to future-proof infrastructure. CapEx tied to AI infrastructure isn’t showing signs of topping; if anything, forecasts may still be too conservative.

Why it matters: Token usage, a proxy for how heavily AI tools are being used, is doubling every six months. That translates directly to more compute, which requires more chips, more power efficiency, and more architectural innovation. In short, the rails are down, and the trains are speeding up.

CapEx Surge as Hyperscalers Ramp Spending into 2025

Beyond General-Purpose Compute: The Rise of Custom Silicon

The architecture of AI is shifting. Specialized chips—ASICs from names like Broadcom, Google (TPUs), and Amazon (Tranium)—are no longer secondary players; they’re core to how hyperscalers are optimizing cost and performance. This has implications up and down the stack. Nvidia’s pivot toward more tailored inference solutions is part of this evolution.

Meanwhile, fabless semiconductor designers, often behind the scenes, are developing chips that improve bandwidth, reduce power consumption, and unlock efficiency across the AI workflow. These innovators may not be household names yet, but their impact on AI deployment is foundational. It’s why we see SMHX as a space with significant long-term potential—it captures the "brains" behind the operation, not just the brawn.

Final Thoughts: AI Is the Story and Semiconductors Are the Backbone

If there’s one takeaway from this quarter’s discussion, it’s this: AI adoption isn’t slowing. It’s broadening. From sovereign initiatives to data centers to consumer internet, the demand for compute is feeding a flywheel of innovation, and semiconductors are at its center. Whether it's the industry giants or the rising specialists, this ecosystem is dynamic, diversified, and far from peaking.

We’ll continue to monitor the landscape closely each quarter. For now, it’s clear: AI and semis are inextricably linked, and the runway ahead is long.

Disclosures

Holdings will vary for the VanEck Semiconductor ETF and its corresponding Index. For a complete list of holdings in the ETF, please click here: SMH - VanEck Semiconductor ETF - Holdings.

Holdings will vary for the VanEck Fabless Semiconductor ETF and its corresponding Index. For a complete list of holdings in the ETF, please click here: SMHX - VanEck Fabless Semiconductor ETF - Holdings.

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

“CapEx” – capital expenditure or capital expense, is the money an organization or corporate entity spends to buy, maintain, or improve its fixed assets, such as buildings, vehicles, equipment, or land. Prior to using any AI tools, please consult your compliance and legal departments to assess and mitigate potential risks associated with its application in your specific regulatory environment.

Please note that any content generated by an Artificial Intelligence system has not been subject to a human review, and thus no assurance can be made as to its accuracy. Please exercise caution when using AI systems and verify the content produced through such systems wherever possible.

An investment in the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) and VanEck Fabless Semiconductor ETF (SMHX) may be subject to risks which include, among others, risks related to investing in the semiconductor industry, special risk considerations of investing in Taiwanese issuers, equity securities, small-, medium and large capitalization companies, foreign securities, emerging market issuers, foreign currency, depositary receipts, issuer-specific changes, market, operational, index tracking, authorized participant concentration, new fund, no guarantee of active trading market, trading issues, passive management, fund shares trading, premium/ discount and liquidity of fund shares, non-diversified, and index-related concentration risks, all of which may adversely affect the Fund. Small, medium and large capitalization companies may be subject to elevated risks. Emerging market issuers and foreign securities may be subject to securities markets, political and economic, investment and repatriation restrictions, different rules and regulations, less publicly available financial information, foreign currency and exchange rates, operational and settlement, and corporate and securities laws risks.

Investing involves substantial risk and high volatility, including possible loss of principal. An investor should consider the investment objective, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus, which contains this and other information, call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com. Please read the prospectus and summary prospectus carefully before investing.

data.title: Realty Income Is Better Than Simon Property After U.S. Credit Rating Downgrade
data.description:
O and SPG stock: Q1 earning reports met U.S. Credit rating

I last analyzed Realty Income (NYSE:O) in late April. That article was titled “Realty Income Q1 Preview: 7% Equity Bond In Disguise”. It rated O as a buy based on its latest dividend declaration and my projection for its Q1 earnings results. Quote:

Realty Income Corporation’s latest dividend declaration and FQ1 earnings revisions have made it a strong candidate as an equity bond. The cash dividend yield is 5.7%, but the AFFO yield is more than 7%. Dividend yields underestimate O’s attractiveness as an equity bond for long-term investors, and AFFO yield is a better metric.

As for Simon Property Group stock (NYSE:SPG), I last covered it in mid-April. That article was titled "Hold Simon Property If You're Sick Of The Tariff Rollercoaster”. I rated SPG as a hold after evaluating the tariff uncertainty at that time.

Since then, a few parameters have changed materials surrounding both these two stocks and also the overall REIT market. The remainder of this article will focus on the top changes in each category as I see. For the former category, I will focus on the business updates they provided in their Q1 2025 earnings reports (ER). For the latter category, I will evaluate the likelihood of persisting high interest rates given the macroscopic developments since my last writings. After examining these changes, I keep seeing O as a better-round REIT stock than SPG, thus maintaining my buy rating for O and hold rating for SPG.

Let me start with their latest earnings reported in Q1 ER. As seen, both company announced their Q1 ER in early May, and both companies faced some challenges in the first quarter. To wit, SPG's Normalized FFO came in at $2.67, a miss by $0.24. Similarly, O's Normalized FFO came in at $1.05, also missing the consensus estimate but by a much smaller margin of $0.01. I do see several macroscopic headwinds facing both of them (and also the REIT sector in general) given the series of geopolitical developments ongoing, such as concerning inflation data, trade disputes, and most recently, the downgrade of the U.S. credit rating. I expect these changes to keep borrowing elevated in the future and keep pressuring O and SPG, and I further expect the pressure to be more severe on SPG, as detailed next.

SPG stock uses more leverage than O

The above macroeconomic developments have caused interest rates to increase noticeably since my last writings on both stocks. Take the 10-year treasury bond rates as an example (see the next chart), the reading has increased from as low as 4% in April 2025 to the current level of 4.37%. In particular, when Moody’s Ratings announced the downgrade of the U.S. credit rating from AAA to AA1, 10-year rates responded immediately with an increase pushing past 4.5%. Treasury rates with longer durations showed my pronounced increased. For example, 30-year treasury rates temporarily pushed over 5%.

I do see good reasons for Moody’s downgrade given the budget situation in the U.S. and I expect the downgrade to cause elevated borrowing rates to sustain longer. My thought process here is detailed in a recent article (when I analyze the impact of the credit downgrade on gold) and the gist is quoted below for ease of reference:

These uncertainties could cause a range of potential long-term impacts, including the loss of investor confidence, the reduced demand for U.S. assets, and also higher borrowing costs for the U.S. government. But the impact that I fear the most is the erosion of U.S. dollar’s global reserve status. While immediate and drastic changes are unlikely, a credit rating downgrade could weaken the dollar's status as the world's primary reserve currency over the long-term. This could cause other countries go gradually diversify away from dollar-denominated reserves.

All the above effects can lead to higher borrowing rates in the long term. As such, I prefer REIT stocks that rely less on debt financing. And O is the one with lower leverage – much lower in my view – than SPG, judging by the balance sheet data reported in their Q1 ERS. As seen, O's Total Debt to Equity is about 69.96% as of Q1, a stark contrast to SPG's high 773.47%. Other metrics paint the same picture. For instance, O's Long-Term Debt/Total Capital is 37.76%, again considerably lower than SPG's 78.96%. Furthermore, O's Debt/Cash Flow ratio of 12.36 is also noticeably lower than SPG’s 14.74.

O and SPG stock: valuation comparison

In terms of valuation, SPG and O are similarly priced in terms of their price to FFO ratios. O is priced at a FWD P/FFO ratio of 13.12x as of this writing, slightly above SPG’s FWD P/FFO ratio of 12.95x. However, I think such a comparison could be misleading for several reasons.

First, the above valuation comparison does not take into consideration their different capital structures and the use of leverages as mentioned above. When compared in valuation metrics that are adjusted for leverages, O is actually cheaper than SPG, as you can see from the following table.

Second, the above comparison also lacks the historical context, as provided in the next chart below. As seen, SPG’s current Price/cash flow ratio of 13.55 is essentially on par with its long-term average of 13.57, suggesting a fair valuation compared to historical standard. In contrast, O's current Price/cash flow ratio of 13.68 is notably below its long-term average of 17.48, indicating a significant discount compared to its historical valuation.

Finally, for stocks who have a long history of paying dividends (which is the case for both O and SPG), I consider dividend payouts a much more reliable indicator of their true owners’ earnings than accounting earnings. Consequently, I consider the dividend yield a much more accurate valuation measure than valuation multiples based on accounting earnings. Along this line of thought, the following chart shows the dividend yield spread between O and the SPG in the past 5 years since 2021. As seen, the current value of this spread is 0.566%. The average spread of is -1.299% over the past 5 years. As such, the current spread is far above the historical average and actually is among the highest levels in this period, indicating an unusually attractive valuation for O when compared to SPG.

Other risks and final thoughts

O entails some downside risks. One of my key arguments above is that it’s more conservatively leveraged than SPG. However, the operative word here is relative. The company is also quite leveraged under the broader scheme of things. For example, its total debt swelled considerable as of Q1 compared to its 2024 levels (by more than 25%) largely due to the Spirit acquisition. When combined with the changes in borrowing rates, its interest expense rose even more YOY (by almost 40%). External acquisition has been an important driver for O’s past growth. Its current debt burden and my projection of higher borrowing rates could restrict this strategy going forward.

As for SPG, there are plenty of upside risks too. SPG focuses on owning and operating premier, high-quality retail real estate, specifically Class A malls and Premium Outlets. These properties often benefit from higher foot traffic and sales per square foot. They also often demonstrate better resilience during market downturns. For example, the company’s metrics have been generally favorable in recent quarters, despite the ongoing geopolitical uncertainty. The occupancy rates were actually slightly better in the recent 1~2 quarters compared to the like period a year ago. I expect the rates to hold between 95.5% and 96.5% in the 1~2 two years.

Let me close with a big picture with the following market sector dashboard (BTW, it’s a downloadable Google Sheet). This dashboard is a tool our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) developed to help our members read the overall market condition. As seen, the REIT sector represented by VNQ is one of the most attractively valued sectors in terms of dividend yield Z-score. However, I think the sector is attractively valued for a good reason, given my macroscopic uncertainties that culminated in the U.S. credit rating downgrade. As such, I urge investors to exercise caution in their choices of specific REIT stocks, despite the overall attractive valuation of the sector. For us, we prefer companies with lower leverage and proven models during different economic cycles. These considerations have made O a better choice in our view than SPG.

data.title: RWR: Diversification In Name Only
data.description:
Contradiction: Diversification in Sector Focused ETFs

Ray Dalio, founder of Bridgewater Associates, is known for many things. One aspect of investing he is well known for is his emphasis on asset diversification. Dalio is quoted as saying, "diversifying well is the most important thing you need to do in order to invest well." No doubt it may seem strange then, or even a contradiction, to be discussing diversification in a sector specific ETF like SPDR® Dow Jones® REIT ETF (NYSEARCA:RWR). Indeed, a sector specific ETF inherently limits diversification, concentrating only on a specific industry or theme. Prospective RWR investors have already accepted that industry concentration. Nonetheless, sector-specific ETFs, like RWR, construct their funds to limit concentration of assets into specific tickers (names) and REIT sectors (e.g. industrial, residential etc).

A Primer: SPDR® Dow Jones® REIT ETF

Financials

According to its website, RWR seeks to provide exposure to publicly traded REIT securities in the U.S. It does this by tracking Dow Jones U.S. Select REIT Capped Index, which is designed to provide broad exposure to publicly traded real estate securities/markets. Year to date, RWR has returned 0.96% compared to 1.0% YTD for its benchmark.

Additional fund characteristics are provided in the table below

Price/FFO 19.00
Price/NAV 0.94
Fund Distribution Yield 3.88%
Gross Expense Ratio 0.25%

Peer Comparison

Over the last year, RWR has underperformed its near peer "REIT only" ETFs. RWR returned 6.75% over the year vs comparable REIT ETFs like USRT (9.19%) and SCHH (7.61%). All three ETFs track different indicies, RWR tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Select REIT Capped Index (see above), USRT tracks the FTSE Nareit Equity REITS Index and SCHH tracks the Dow Jones Equity All REIT Capped Index.

From a yield perspective, RWR currently yields around 3.9%, roughly inline with ETF peers. USRT (yields as of 30 Apr 2025) i3.25% and SCHH 3.18%. However, RWR's expense ratio of 0.25% is notably higher than USRT (0.08%) and SCHH (0.07%). All 3 REIT ETFs rebalance quarterly.

In summary, RWR has higher fees than its peers, similar yield, but its 1yr return has lagged behind.

Ticker and Sector Concentration

In analyzing RWR, we focus on its weighting methodology, concentration of it stop holdings and overall sector allocations, factors critical to understanding REIT ETFs. We also evaluate its effectiveness in achieving diversification, considering both top holdings and sector-level exposures. RWR’s weighting methodology is based on market capitalization. Larger REITs carry greater weight, but there are built-in constraints. For example, no individual company may exceed 10% of the ETF’s total assets. This strategy seeks to mitigate excessive risks associated with individual stocks and promotes balanced sector exposure within the publicly traded space. These rules limit RWR's concentration in specific names/tickers; spreading out investor dollars across 103 holdings (as of 30 May 2025).

The ETFs top 10 holdings (as of 30 May 2025) are pictured in Figure 2. All in all, the top 10 holdings comprise 50% of the overall ETF weighting. RWR also seeks diversification through its sector allocation as shown in Figure 3. The weights reflect market capitalization of the respective REIT sectors.

Figure 2 and Figure 3 are fairly standard data points for any REIT ETF. RWR is concentrated in about 10 major names, but limits concentration through exposure to a 103 individual names and multiple REIT sectors.

Risk of Geographic Concentration

Real estate’s enduring maxim—“location, location, location”—underscores that value is welded to geography. Buildings cannot simply relocate, so any ETF comprised of companies that generate their profits from brick and mortar inherit the local economic, regulatory, and climate risks of the ground beneath each asset. RWR's prospectus highlights potential risks related to concentration, clearly stating that the ETF may become significantly weighted in specific geographic areas or property sectors due to the nature of its underlying REIT holdings. But the ETF rules do nothing to mitigate this risk. From our perspective, geographic risk matters for REITs due to their physical property investments.

To evaluate RWR's geographic diversification, we analyzed the property distribution of its largest holdings at the state level, which comprise around 50% of RWR's total weighting. Our analysis uncovers a significant degree of geographic concentration rather than widespread diversification.

Texas, for instance, accounts for roughly 13.2% of the property exposure from these top holdings. Florida follows with approximately 8.7%, and California contributes around 7.4%. Combined, these three states represent nearly 30% of the total weighted property exposure. Including the next two states—Georgia (4.6%) and Ohio (4.3%)—raises this percentage even further. Clearly, the ETF’s real estate exposure is dominated by a limited number of states. Despite an apparent broad diversification on the surface, these concentrated risks could pose unexpected vulnerabilities to investors. Specifically, a natural disaster that strikes one or more states (e.g. hurricanes in Texas and Florida).

Risks

Several factors could challenge our thesis. Interestingly enough, as of 2024, California, Texas, and Florida collectively contribute approximately 29.3% of the United States' total Gross Domestic Product (GDP). Individually, California accounts for 14.14%, Texas for 9.34%, and Florida for 5.82% of the national GDP. So RWR's geographic concentration mimics (in aggregate) these states contributions to overall U.S. GDP. However, with an "underweight" of California and New York and "overweight" of Texas. Now, some investors may like that reversal and see RWR as really a proxy for U.S. economic performance with a Texas overweight. So all in all, RWR just reflects a general investment in the U.S. economy. Moreover, the academic literature on geographic concentration risk in passive REIT ETFs is still limited, leaving little consensus on whether geographic concentration systematically harms long‑term returns. Additionally, what is the standard for "geographic diversification"? There is no consensus on what would make one ETF "geographically concentrated" vs "geographically diversified". Any of these factors could weaken the foundation of our thesis.

Conclusion

Ultimately, although RWR offers investors an efficient method to gain broad exposure to real estate and the returns that it offers, we believe that it contains risk that classic volatility measures and other traditional investor tools do not capture, what we are calling geographic risk. Until an alternative vehicle is created that tracks concentrations of geographic risk. We follow Mr. Dalio’s advice to “diversify well.” For REIT investing, that means emphasizing geographic diversification.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Back Above 6000
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 closed above 6000 for the first time since mid-February. The index posted its second consecutive weekly gain, finishing up 1.5% from the previous Friday, and is now sitting 2.34% below its record close from February 19th, 2025.

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached a then all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53.

This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new then all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above the 50-day moving average since May 1st and above the 200-day moving average since May 12th. Additionally, the 200-day moving average has been above the 50-day moving average since April 14th.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range.

On April 9th, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%). Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 1.03%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is market cap-weighted index which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 2.25% year to date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 2.05% year to date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Circle Soars Nearly 250% After Completing $1+ Billion IPO
data.description:
Two sizable issuers went public this past week and featured the best first-day pop for a billion-dollar IPO in Renaissance’s 30+ year history. Three IPOs and two SPACs also submitted initial filings.

Stablecoin issuer Circle Internet Group (CRCL) priced a further upsized offering above the raised range to raise $1.1 billion at a $7.9 billion market cap. Circle’s US dollar-denominated payment stablecoin USDC is the #2 ranking stablecoin, holding nearly one-third of the market. The company is poised to benefit from tailwinds in the crypto space, as well as pending legislation that will provide regulatory clarity. It will likely face obstacles like declining rates and high distribution costs, but public investors were eager to dive in, with the company soaring 168% on day one. Circle traded up further from there to finish the week with a 247% gain.

Chronic care management platform Omada Health (OMDA) priced the midpoint to raise $150 million at a $1.2 billion market cap. Originally focused solely on diabetes prevention and weight health, Omada now offers various cardiometabolic programs, a physical therapy program, GLP-1 Care Tracks, and behavioral health support. The company has demonstrated growth and retention, though the growing popularity of GLP-1 therapies could threaten its cardiometabolic offerings. Omada finished up 21%.

2 IPOs During the Week of June 2nd, 2025
Issuer Business Deal Size Market Cap at IPO Price vs. Midpoint First Day Return Return at 06/06

Circle (CRCL)

 $1,054M $7,898M 24% +168% +247%
Cryptocurrency issuer behind the USDC stablecoin.

Omada Health (OMDA)

 $150M $1,217M 0% +21% +21%
Provides digital health tools for patients with chronic conditions.

Three IPOs submitted initial filings this week. Latin American gold and copper miner Aura Minerals (AUGO) filed for a $100 million IPO. Southeast US-focused community bank CoastalSouth Bancshares (OTCPK:COSO) filed to raise $80 million. Hong Kong gift basket company Gifts International (GINT) filed to raise $6 million at a $79 million market cap.

Two SPAC also submitted initial filings. HCM III Acquisition (HCMAU), the third blank check company formed by Hondius Capital Management, filed to raise $220 million. Cantor Equity Partners II (CEPC.RC), the twelfth blank check company formed by Cantor Fitzgerald, filed to raise $200 million.

9 Filings During the Week of June 2nd, 2025
Issuer Business Deal Size Sector Lead Underwriter

Aura Minerals (AUGO)

Gold and copper miner with operations throughout Latin America.

Cantor Equity Partners II (CEPC.RC)

Twelfth blank check company formed by Cantor Fitzgerald.

CoastalSouth Bancshares (COSO)

 $80M Financials Piper Sandler
Community bank serving South Carolina and Georgia.

Gifts International (GINT)

 $6M Consumer Discretionary R. F. Lafferty
Hong Kong-based designer and distributor of gift packages.

HCM III Acquisition (HCMAU)

 $220M SPAC Cantor Fitz.
Third blank check company formed by Hondius Capital Management.

Oxley Bridge Acquisition (OBAU)

 $220M SPAC Cantor Fitz.
Blank check company targeting the global consumer and technology sectors.

Beta FinTech (BTFT)

 $10M Financials Cathay Securities
Hong Kong-based provider of securities placement and other financial services.

TryHard Holdings (THH)

 $7M Communication Services US Tiger Securities
Provides live event services and operates restaurants in Japan.

C&K Group (CKG.RC)

 $7M Consumer Discretionary Revere Sec.
Hong Kong-based jeweler focused on pearls.

Week Ahead

Three sizable IPOs are currently scheduled to list in the week ahead, and some small names may also join the calendar throughout the week.

Mobile banking platform Chime (CHYM) plans to raise $800 million at a $10.5 billion market cap. Chime says it has 8.6 million Active Members using its suite of products, with 67% of them relying on Chime to serve as their primary financial relationship as of March 31, 2025. The company believes this positions it as its members' central financial hub, making Chime the platform through which members consistently deposit their paychecks and conduct their everyday spend. In the first quarter of 2025, its Active Members used Chime for an average of 54 transactions per month, of which 75% were purchase transactions using Chime-branded debit and credit cards. The company previously raised funding at a $25 billion market value. Chime’s revenue growth accelerated to 32% in the 1Q25, although the majority of its revenue comes from economically sensitive consumer spending.

Space and defense company Voyager Technologies (VOYG) plans to raise $303 million at a $1.6 billion market cap. The company’s business spans three segments: Defense & National Security, which provides communications technologies, guidance, navigation and controls, signals intelligence, and defense systems; Space Solutions, which delivers space infrastructure, advanced space technology, science systems, and mission services; and Starlab Space Stations, which is a commercial space station planned to succeed the ISS. Key partners and customers include Palantir, NASA, Lockheed Martin, the US Air Force, and Sierra Space. The company has a $900 million ceiling IDIQ contract with the U.S. Airforce, although it is significantly unprofitable, and its loss margin worsened in the 1Q25.

SMB-focused insurer Ategrity Specialty Insurance (ASIC) plans to raise $100 million at a $719 million market cap. Ategrity is a specialty insurance company dedicated to providing excess and surplus products to SMBs across the US. As of March 31, 2025, the company had a network of 512 distribution partners, providing increased transaction opportunities and diversified sources of business. It offers insurance solutions for property (40% of gross written premiums in 2024) and casualty (60%), Ategrity wrote $437 million in gross written premiums in 2024 and had a combined ratio of 93.9%. It had total assets of $1.1 billion and equity of $398 million at year-end. Its gross written premium increased 24% in 2024, although it depends on its largest shareholder for investment management.

Street research is expected for four companies in the week ahead, and three lock-up periods will be expiring.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 6/5/2025, the Renaissance IPO Index was down 0.6% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 was up 1.6%. Renaissance Capital's IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Arm Holdings (ARM) and Corebridge Financial (CRBG). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 18.2% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 16.0%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Galderma and Amman Mineral

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ATRenew: Old Phones, New Profits
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Today we're looking at a dominant Chinese player in the recycling industry of used electronics: ATRenew Inc. (NYSE:RERE). Due to their impressive revenue growth (+27% y/y), operational efficiency, low CapEx business model and increase in free cash flow, ATRenew is positioned well to benefit from the upcoming recycling boom.

In this analysis, we will look at what makes them an attractive investment and dive deep into the valuation of their business.

ATRenew Inc. At A Glance

The company was founded in 2011 and is headquartered in Shanghai. ATRenew's mission is to give a second life to all idle goods of consumer electronics by facilitating recycling and trade-in services, and distributing the devices to prolong their lifecycle. As of 2024, they have around 2,100 employees with an annual revenue of $2.2 billion and recycle around 35 million consumer devices per year. But let's walk along the timeline real quick:

An important milestone was the strategic partnership with JD.com (NYSE:JD) and Xiaomi (NYSE:XIACF) in 2015 that enabled trade-in programs.

2017: Opening of their first automated operation center in Changzhou, which was important to scale.

2018: Launching their B2B trading platform for pre-owned electronics.

2019: Integrating into JD.com's Paipai marketplace for consumer electronics.

2021: NYSE IPO; raising $261 million at a $3.6 billion valuation.

2023: Collaboration with Apple (NYSE:AAPL) for its recycling and trade-in services.

Recycling Market Outlook And ATRenew's Standpoint

First, we need to take a look at the broader market and industry ATRenew is operating in because even a good company can go bad in a market that will be obsolete 10 years from now. China was setting the 5-year goal to become a more circular economy in 2021 to increase the lifespan of products. Therefore, having a mission as a company that is backed by politics and the public is a good start.

To put this into perspective, China discards around 160 million electronic devices per year (reuse, recycling, landfill). This whooping number inherits immense value that is thrown away, and therefore can be translated into economic value by refurbishing the still usable ones and bringing them back into the economy. By already knowing that ATRenew recycles around 35 million devices per year, we see that they're a sizable player in that field. The expected compounded annual growth rate of 14.5% for the Chinese electronic waste recycling market until 2035 reflects that there will be definitely no shortage of electronic devices.

To sum up, we can say that the market trends look favorable and the industry they operate in won't be obsolete for the foreseeable future.

Economic Snapshot

Next, to evaluate the company properly it's important to understand their history of revenues, cash flows and debt. This gives us the clear picture about the economics of that company and whether it's shareholder value destructing or creating.

Revenue

First, we see their revenue history shows some impressive growth:

This is the impact of the rapid growing circular economy market that results in 20%-plus revenue growth. Also, their recent Q1 filing is showing no sign of slowing down that growth rate with a 27.5% revenue increase year over year.

Cash Flows

What's most important for us to be sure is that revenue is converted into operating- and then free cash flow. Because that's the most relevant for us - cash that can be either distributed, used for share buybacks or increase the book value of the company. All three enhancing shareholder returns.

Now to ATRenew's cash flow situation:

This is a very important graph because it shows the ability to generate substantial operating- (blue) and free cash flow (purple) for us owners. But why is operating cash flow so important again?

The operating cash flow tells us exactly how much of our revenue is converted into real cash from our operations that we can use for investments and financing things like buying back shares, dividends or repaying debt. All other numbers like EBIT and earnings are relatively easy to trick around from an accounting standpoint. In cash flow, it's impossible to fake things because you look at the bank account on the first day of the year and the end of the year. Cash is cash. In the case of ATRenew, their operations are already cash flow positive, which is the heart beat of every company. If you're not cash flow positive with your operations, your business will fail over the long run because you constantly need to invest and if your operations are not paying for these investments, some day other lenders won't do as well.

Here it still looks like ATRenew is sorting out their margin profile due to a bit of changing operating cash flow, but overall, it's very good that they were able to stay positive over the last three years and currently don't need extra cash to fund their operations.

But what is free cash flow? Free cash flow is the most important metric for us investors. By far and forever. Free cash flow subtracts the capital spending and investments your business needs to bring up every year to keep up with competitors and grow the operating cash flow. Thus, operating cash flow minus investments for operations. Everything that is left now is free cash flow. This is the money the company can use for dividends, share repurchases or repaying debt obligations.

I always like to use the analogy of buying an apartment for investing purposes here: You pay a price to get a rental cash flow. What you have left after all the rental expenses per year is your free cash flow of a property. Same for businesses. In our example here, ATRenew was able to generate $224 million in free cash flow over the last three years, or $75 million on average per year. That's quite good.

Net Debt

Companies that run on a large amount of debt inherit some serious risks because before you get the free cash flow as an owner, other parties want to be paid also and can even shut down the company if the business is not able to repay that debt. Therefore, I prefer companies with manageable debt profiles, that won't conclude in big problems for us owners.

But what is manageable? The best is, if the company has a higher cash position that it owes money to others. Resulting in net cash. This is the absolute best and safest because they're always able to repay all of their debt just by their cash on hand. But not every company is structured like this, of course.

Let's have a look how ATRenew's net debt or maybe cash looks like:

And here we go! Resulting in a net cash position of $319 million as of 2024, ATRenew has exactly what we want! This net cash position means that if we buy the whole company right now, we get a cash surplus of $319 million compared to their debt obligations, which is amazing.

Now we're close to having everything we need to calculate a Fair Value of ATRenew, but before, let's get an understanding of their shareholder structure.

Shareholder structure

From understanding the history of the shareholder structure, we get a feeling of what other big investors think about the company over time and who's in the boat with us. In our case, it looks quite promising:

It shows that institutional investors are not selling out their shares and do believe strongly in their position.

That is also reflected in the top 15 holders that are even accumulating some shares in recent history:

From a shareholder base standpoint, that's what we like to see, being alongside with some other big investors.

Fair Value of ATRenew

Just like with apartment houses, in investing we pay a price to get a certain amount of cash flows. If we want to know if an investment is an attractive one we must put these two things into perspective.

Price

First, let's calculate the price for ATRenew that we really have to pay. To get a fair price of a company we must factor in the debt and all the obligations a company owes to others because if we would just look at the market cap as a price indicator, company A and B with a market cap of $100 million would be the same even if company A has zero debt and B $300 million. Thus, company B is much more expensive than it first looks if we factor in the debt.

The better metrics therefore is Enterprise Value that factors in a company's net debt or cash position. If a company's market cap is $100 million and its net debt is $300 million, the Enterprise Value is $400 million.

In the case of ATRenew it's the following:

Now we know that the Enterprise Value of ATRenew stands at $248 million, so let's continue calculating their average free cash flow for us owners to put this number into some perspective.

Free Cash Flow

We already know that from the Cash flow section that the company is already free cash flow positive for three consecutive years, which is great. But one thing that is also very important to add to the typical way of calculating free cash flow is to look at the equity-based compensation for management.

It makes sense to pay management also with some shares to make sure they are aligned with our shareholder interests, but it's also important to note that every new issued share is diluting our existing ones. Therefore, I always treat share-based compensation like the expense it is to us shareholders and subtract it from free cash flow. That's how we get an even clearer picture of the true situation for us investors. Because what does $1 billion in free cash flow do us good, if the company is diluting shares by $2 billion per year?

In the case of ATRenew, everything looks healthy. They give out some shares to management which is important and the right thing to do, but it's not crazy much so we still get around $50 million in actual free cash flow per year.

Fair Value Conclusion

Now we have both important numbers, we need to put this into perspective and finally get our answer if this is an attractive price right now or not.

And we finally got it. The 4.8 means, that it would take the company 4.8 years to "earn back" our initial investment in free cash flow. This translates into an implied yield of 21% which is quite attractive. In other words: The company earns you 21 cents in free cash flow on every dollar of your initial investment per year.

To give you some sort of price target I want to calculate that by applying a 15 times multiple to their EV/FCF multiple, which is seen as a fair average by most investors.

Therefore, we calculate the new enterprise value first:

Our fair Enterprise Value translates into a Market Cap of $1,099 million.

To see what our potential upside is we put the current Market Cap of $567 million and the calculated Fair Value Market Cap of $1,099 million into perspective and voilà: Our upside potential.

From our current price point the potential price increase to reflect fair value results in a ≈94% upside or a $4.98 price target per share.

Shareholder Return Initiatives

The two major ways to return cash to shareholders is either through dividends or share buybacks. ATRenew doesn't pay any dividends, but they do have a share repurchase program in place.

"During the first quarter of 2025, ATRenew repurchased a total of approximately 0.4 million ADSs for approximately US$1.2 million under its current share repurchase program, which authorizes the Company to repurchase up to US$50 million worth of its shares (including ADSs) through June 27, 2025. As of March 31, 2025, the Company had repurchased a total of approximately 10.7 million ADSs for approximately US$27.1 million under this share repurchase program." - ATRenew's Q1 2025 earnings release.

That means under this ongoing share repurchase program there is ≈$23mm left and due to its ending on June 27, 2025, I believe the board will come up with a new one to take advantage of the currently low and attractive share price.

Risks And Uncertainties

Every investment inherits some risks that we have to factor in.

In the case of Chinese companies, one is definitely the ongoing political tensions between the USA and China and some unknown outputs that this conflict might bring us. Therefore, this is a risk that is very hard to assume possible outcomes from or put some numbers behind.

Another risk is that ATRenew is still in its growth phase and it's not certain that these cash flows will stay the same. Even though my personal view is that they have the worst behind and are on a good way to stay cash flow positive over the long run, we can't be 100% sure.

An additional risk is the ADS and delisting risk that has been ongoing for years now. An ADS is a U.S.-traded security that represents shares in a foreign company. It allows investors in the U.S. to buy shares in an international company without having to trade on foreign exchanges. Due to recent delisting risks, these ADS might not be able to continue trading at NYSE or a major US stock exchange and might have to switch to an Over-the-counter 'OTC' exchange. For us, small investors, that's nothing really bad because we are not forced to sell because of regulations and can continue trading at the OTC exchange. But large funds like pension funds or ETFs are required by charter or compliance to only hold listed securities that meet strict regulatory oversight. Therefore, they would be forced to sell certain securities and therefore probably create a decline in price over the short term, which we even could turn into an opportunity.

Investment Conclusion

ATRenew is a dominant player with a bright outlook, manageable risks, a management team that is aligned with shareholder interests, strong free cash flows and a share repurchase plan in place, that will benefit shareholders. I value this company as a long-term portfolio addition due to its unique position and economics. Given the potential upside between the fair value calculation and its current trading price, I rate this as a 'Buy'.

data.title: DXP Enterprises: Hold Rating As Macro Challenges Offset EBITDA Upside
data.description:
Key Thesis

We initiate on DXP Enterprises, Inc. (NASDAQ:DXPE) at Hold, $92 PT. DXP Enterprises is a leading distributor of MRO products and services with diversified exposure to energy and industrial infrastructure in the US and Canada. Here’s the investment paradox: we are modeling FY25E EBITDA of $212mn—55%+ above The street consensus—on a record IPS backlog, high-margin water/wastewater M&A, and better-than-expected Q1/25 run-rate. Management’s tariff pass-through ability affords DXPE critical pricing power in a volatile MRO landscape.

But this robust backdrop is offset by a complex macro picture: ongoing manufacturing contraction, elevated raw material costs, and sticky inflation argue for multiple restraint. Our $92 PT assumes 6.4× forward EBITDA; above DXPE’s historical median, but materially discounted to peers. We believe this is prudent caution in spite of the clearly accelerating fundamentals. Key risks include industrial demand shocks and working capital swings, which could increase the volatility of earnings and capital returns.

Bottom line: though upside to EBITDA is real, our balanced R/R argument supports our Hold rating—investors need macro headwinds to stabilize and multiple re-rating to become constructive.

Margin Profile is Mixed: Water/Wastewater Acquisitions Drive Upside, but Execution and Scale are Key

The margin profile is mixed when looking at DXP Enterprises’ recent bias toward higher-margin water/wastewater acquisitions—acquisitions which carry significant implications for EBITDA growth and durability. The completion of Riordan Materials Corporation and Florida Valve & Equipment Corp. (including Environmental MD) bolsters DXPE’s municipal and industrial water exposure both geographically and from a product capability perspective. Significantly, these businesses generated combined LTM revenues of $14.2mn and LTM adjusted EBITDA of $3.2mn as of March 31, 2023. Such implies an EBITDA margin of roughly 22%, substantially higher than consolidated business average. This suggests a structural rise in margins as water/wastewater becomes a larger part of the mix.

The levers are clear: these platforms offer recurring value-add cycles with entrenched municipal and industrial customers, less susceptible to the cyclicality seen elsewhere in the portfolio. However, investors should be aware that while this margin-higher segment can provide a buffer to the portfolio, there remains uncertainty around the speed with which these benefits can be scaled across the entire business.

Execution risks, absolute contribution to EBITDA is still modest, and follow-on deal flow pace all remain key to watch. Overall, the math suggests DXPE’s water/wastewater acquisition strategy is driving margins higher, and could provide further upside if management’s execution continues to be disciplined. However, the full rerate argument will likely need to show both scalability and consistency of results in a volatile macro environment.

Volatility in working capital muddies FCF visibility

Working capital / cash flow trends are especially relevant to investors in distribution businesses such as DXP Enterprises where swings in inventory / receivables can drive volatility - and, as recent results show, remain a double-edged sword. The full picture is mixed: on one hand, DXPE exited Q1 FY25E strongly on headline metrics; on the other, uncertainty around the balance sheet warrants caution.

For example, the business closed the quarter with $114.3mn in cash against $647.3mn in debt - a net debt to EBITDA ratio of 2.50x - enough to keep working capital discipline in focus for lenders and equity holders alike. FCF, critical for funding M&A and debt repayment, can fluctuate materially depending on inventory purchase and AR collection - which management also calls out as risks, especially in a more uncertain macro environment.

The dynamic is straightforward: strong organic and acquired sales growth (like Q1 FY25E) necessitate higher inventory to support growth and can push up receivable balances, both of which can tie up cash and cause large q/q swings. Even as top-line / EBITDA metrics hit new highs, any material built in working capital / acquisitions can sharply limit FCF generation, limiting balance sheet flexibility and capital return potential.

On balance, the data supports a nuanced risk: earnings momentum is real, but volatility in working capital deployment / FCF generation means investors should temper expectations for smooth, consistent cash returns near term, especially with leverage near the upper end of peer comfort zones.

DXP Enterprises: Above-The Street EBITDA Outlook, with Segments & Tariff Tailwind

We model FY25E EBITDA of $212mn for DXP Enterprises, well above The street at $135mn (-26% y/y), followed by FY26E of $225mn (+6% y/y vs. our base) as we believe The street fundamentally misunderstands both the cadence of the business and recent operational trends. Our conviction is anchored in a dramatically better than feared Q1'25 run-rate - $52.5mn EBITDA (+30% y/y on 15.5% sales growth) - that when annualized sets a $210-220mn base.

Source: FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $1.98B 1 $2.05B 1
EBITDA $134.77M 1 $139.61M 1
Net Income $88.52M 1 $101.88M 1
EPS $5.30 1 $6.10 1
SG&A Expenses $462.04M 1 $478.64M 1

The street's thesis of a Q1 seasonal trough ignores the historical 2H skew (60-65% of annual EBITDA), and discounts a record IPS backlog and margin-accretive add-ons like water/wastewater M&A and digital supply chain ramp. Critically, management is successfully passing through tariffs in MRO, turning industry volatility into a pricing & margin lever.

Risk factors such as an industrial demand shock or working capital swings remain present, but we believe consensus is materially too low given an obvious acceleration in the business and pricing power. Having said that, our operational views are bullish, but upside to EBITDA does not inherently mean a Buy, as multiple and capital allocation remain barriers to rerating.

Valuation

Our $92 PT for DXP Enterprises is driven by a forward EBITDA multiple of 6.4x on our FY26E EBITDA of $225mn (13% implied upside, Hold rating). The multiple represents a premium to DXPE's median historical multiple of 5.8x (59th pctile) but is meaningfully below the peer median of 13.9x (42nd pctile), which reflects a calibrated balancing of:

(1) acknowledgment of accelerating Op momentum (backlog visibility and tariff pass-through underpin our 6.1% FY26E EBITDA growth) and

(2) a deliberate discount for macro headwinds and sector dislocation.

While the market still seems fixated on the near-term risks of industrial softness and RM inflation, we think investors underappreciate the structural margin leverage from high-value water/wastewater integrations, which could drive an outsized multiple rerating if macro relieves faster than consensus expects.

In this context, the multiple of 6.4x appropriately rewards execution and pricing while leaving room for cyclicality and capital return volatility. Ultimately, this valuation framework balances our differentiated above-consensus EBITDA view with the reality of near-term sector dislocation, leading to a more disciplined stance until multiple expansion catalysts emerge.

Risks to our Hold thesis

While our Hold rating on DXP Enterprises reflects a balanced view of structural earnings upside vs sector volatility, key risks could flip our thesis and warrant caution on valuation upside. Most notably, DXPE's earnings trajectory is highly vulnerable to macro shocks in industrial / energy end-markets; if wider industrial production reverses or U.S. PMI fails to sustain, MRO volumes / backlog conversion may disappoint, challenging our above-consensus EBITDA forecast despite currently robust IPS-driven backlog / tariff pass-through momentum.

Additionally, the company's recent pace of deals / inorganic growth ambition raises the risk of integration missteps; execution risk is tangible given prior deal volatility in cost synergies / margin realization, while a surge in working capital (e.g., DSO, inventory) could pressure FCF / delay deleveraging plans.

Investors should closely monitor monthly sales / business-day, incremental margin progression in IPS / SCS, and conversion of backlog into revenue — any sharp deterioration or signs of acquisition integration challenges could flag our thesis as overly optimistic relative to core industrial cyclicality / capital intensity risks.

Conclusion

We believe a Hold rating on DXP Enterprises is the most disciplined stance to take at this point, despite above-consensus conviction into NT EBITDA acceleration and mgmt's realized pricing power. Our stance is informed by operational outperformance and backlog momentum, but we believe valuation upside is capped until macro headwinds ease and the market is rewarded with a higher multiple for these fundamentals. We urge investors to maintain vigilance - the catalyst is the pace of backlog conversion and integration, as any signs of macro stabilization or flawless M&A execution can quickly lay the foundation for a more constructive, upward rerating.

data.title: How Wildfires Impact Canadian Midstream Operations
data.description:
Wildfires burning in Canada’s oil-producing region are prompting investors to examine the potential impact to midstream companies.

There were over 24 out-of-control wildfires in Alberta as of Wednesday. Notably, a significant 470,000 barrels per day of oil production is located within 20 kilometers of these large (>10 hectares) out-of-control wildfires, according to Bloomberg.

There are 210 active fires throughout Canada as of Thursday, according to the country’s interagency fire center. The most fires are currently in the British Columbia province, followed by Alberta. Significantly, Alberta produces much of Canada’s crude oil. Many midstream companies operate in this region as well.

The ongoing wildfires in Alberta are causing concerns about oil supply disruptions, as the country’s daily crude production had been curtailed by about 7% as of Tuesday, Reuters reported. Some producers had evacuated non-essential workers and temporarily curtailed production, but the situation is still evolving.

Second-quarter midstream volumes could be impacted if midstream companies and producers temporarily shut down operations due to wildfires. However, further investigation is necessary to assess the potential impact. Factors such as the duration of the fires, extent of infrastructure damage, and effectiveness of response measures will influence the overall outcome. Rain earlier this week has reportedly allowed some producers to restore curtailed volumes, per Bloomberg.

Given the developing situation, it’s worth considering the parallels to the June 2023 wildfires. During that period, despite some temporary shutdowns and operational challenges, the midstream sector did not experience a significantly pronounced impact.

At the time, midstream companies such as TC Energy (TRP), Keyera Corporation (KEY), and Pembina Pipeline Corporation (PPL) each temporarily halted operations due to wildfires. In each example, the company did not experience any significant impact and was able to restart operations.

Canadian Midstream Exposure

Canadian midstream companies can be of interest to investors given their strong dividend track records and defensive qualities. Three Canadian midstream names — South Bow Corp (SOBO), Enbridge (ENB), and TC Energy (TRP) — have year-to-date total returns above 10%, while the S&P 500 is slightly positive and the broader U.S. energy sector is negative.

Investors can gain exposure to Canadian midstream companies through the Alerian Energy Infrastructure ETF (ENFR), which is based on the Alerian Midstream Energy Select Index (AMEI). The fund is a composite of North American midstream energy infrastructure companies. It includes C-corps and MLPs, and companies engaged in the pipeline transportation, storage, and processing of energy commodities.

ENFR is the lowest-cost ETF in the midstream category and is up 4.4% year to date through June 5, handily outpacing the S&P 500 and the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
